race.name: Cycling Shimanami
race.tagline: Japan's premier international cycling event across the Seto Inland Sea

vitals.location: Imabari, Ehime Prefecture to Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Japan

climate.description: Late October in the Seto Inland Sea region features mild autumn conditions with low precipitation risk and light winds, ideal for cycling.

features: Relatively flat route crossing nine bridges that link six islands across the Seto Inland Sea, featuring dedicated blue-line cycling paths with ocean views throughout

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 74. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 70.

course_description.character: A scenic, relatively flat island-hopping route across nine iconic bridges spanning the Seto Inland Sea. The Shimanami Kaido connects six islands (Oshima, Omishima, Hakatajima, Innoshima, Ikuchi-jima, and Mukaishima) between Imabari on Shikoku Island and Onomichi in Hiroshima Prefecture. Dedicated blue-line cycling paths, 150+ rest oases, and comprehensive aid stations with local food and cultural experiences characterize the route. The event allows cyclists unique access to the normally car-only expressway main road, creating an unforgettable atmosphere with closed roads, cheering locals, and photographers lining the route.
course_description.signature_challenge: The nine bridge crossings themselves, particularly the longest bridge onto Shikoku Island, which require sustained effort but reward with spectacular Seto Inland Sea vistas. The optional Shiratakiyama climb offers the most significant elevation challenge with stone kami (gods) statues lining the ascent.
suffering_zones: Shiratakiyama viewpoint climb (optional but challenging, offering panoramic island chain views)
Kumakogen countryside section with technical turns and relentless inclines (27 km course variant)
Omogo-kei Gorge passage with elevation changes
UFO Line section with distant ocean views and sustained climbing

logistics.airport: MYJ (Matsuyama Airport); HIJ (Hiroshima Airport); KIX (Osaka International Airport (Kansai)); FUK (Fukuoka Airport)
logistics.lodging_strategy: Multiple lodging options available through event partnerships, including traditional onsen (hot spring) accommodations. Bike-into-room lodging available at select properties. Luggage transfer services connect accommodations along the route.
logistics.transport: Exceptionally well-organized event with comprehensive support for international participants. Registration opens April 2026. Multiple distance options accommodate varying abilities. Bike rentals available at 10 terminals throughout the route with drop-off flexibility. Luggage forwarding and accommodation partnerships facilitate multi-day touring.

history.origin_story: Cycling Shimanami is Japan's largest organized cycling event, held biennially on the Shimanami Kaido route. The event showcases the scenic Seto Inland Sea crossing and has become an international destination event attracting cyclists from around the world.
notable_moments: Event established as international cycling showcase for Shimanami Kaido route
Biennial schedule established to manage participation and maintain event quality
2026 edition marks continued growth with expanded international promotion through partnerships with cycling organizations including Hawai'i Bicycling League
Event features unique access to normally car-only expressway, available only during the cycling event

biased_opinion.summary: Cycling Shimanami represents the gold standard for mass-participation cycling events in Asia. The combination of world-class organization, stunning scenery, and genuine cultural integration makes it exceptional for riders seeking an unforgettable experience rather than a competitive challenge. The event's biennial schedule and international draw create a unique atmosphere where thousands of cyclists from around the world share the road simultaneously, with locals cheering from sidewalks and photographers lining the route. The flat terrain and excellent support infrastructure make it accessible to a broad range of abilities, though this also means it lacks the physical severity that hardcore cyclists might seek. For those prioritizing logistics, experience quality, and community engagement over raw difficulty, this is a premier destination event.

final_verdict.one_liner: Japan's most prestigious international cycling event offering unparalleled logistics, scenery, and cultural immersion across the Seto Inland Sea.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Cycling Shimanami 2026 stands as a masterclass in event organization and cyclist-centric infrastructure. The event's strength lies not in physical difficulty but in its holistic experience: stunning bridge crossings, dedicated cycling paths, exceptional volunteer support, and authentic cultural integration. The October timing provides ideal weather, and the biennial schedule creates genuine international community. However, the relatively flat terrain and moderate distances mean this event appeals primarily to experience-seekers and recreational cyclists rather than those seeking competitive challenge or significant physical suffering. The comprehensive logistics—including bike rentals, luggage forwarding, and 150+ aid stations—eliminate typical cycling event friction points. For international cyclists planning a Japan cycling pilgrimage, this is non-negotiable; for those seeking a hard cycling challenge, look elsewhere.

videos.title: 10 Things you should know BEFORE you Cycle the Shimanami Kaido in Japan!
videos.description:
Cycle the most beautiful coastal scenery route in Japan!  CNN Travel wrote that this is one of the most incredible cycling routes in the world!  And surprisingly, it is still the best-kept secret in Japan!  Not many Japanese are familiar with the Shimanami Kaido cycling route from Onomichi, Hiroshima to Imabari, Ehime in Shikoku.  In this video, I will cover 10-18 different observations and suggestions on what you should know before you attempt to cycle the Shimanami Kaido.  There are a lot of YouTube videos about cycling, vlogging, and experiencing the Shimanami Kaido, but none of them cover in detail what you should know if this is your first time cycling the Shimanami. This video tries to cover everything that I noticed and wish I knew before I started cycling the Shimanami Kaido for three days.

The entire Shimanami Kaido one-way route is 75 kilometers, crossing over six islands and six massive suspension bridges.  The last bridge before Imabari, the Kurushima Kaikyo Bridge, is the world's longest suspension bridge at four kilometers long.  If you are planning to visit Japan or presently live in Japan and enjoy cycling, the "Shimanami Kaido" is a must on your bucket list!

Other Shimanami Kaido series videos (5 total):

#1:  Part 1 - Ready to cycle the Shimanami Kaido? Getting there and preparing for the cycling ride of your life!
https://youtu.be/-JVJcQJELs0

#2:  Part 2- The Real Story of Riding the Shimanami Kaido with all my problems and mistakes.
https://youtu.be/J2VyN4XuCy8

#3   Part 3 - Cycling the Shimanami Kaido:  Going Home!  
https://youtu.be/BpGd-82EXCM

#4 of the 5 video series: 10 Things to Know BEFORE you cycle the Shimanami Kaido!  
https://youtu.be/Stc7y4m9xOE

#5 of the 5 video series: A MUST for English speakers and the key to a successful Shimanami Kaido!
https://youtu.be/F9Rs0tMgwuw

A shout-out to Taro @sc_dreamin for the E-Bike photos and bicycle rental information.  Taro is an amazing cyclist who has completed the full 75 kilometers of the Shimanani Kaido in just three hours!  He works part-time as a bike mechanic at the Onomichi Rental Shop, and also is a cycling guide on the Shinamami Kaido,  all known as "The coolest cycling guide in Shimanami Kaido."  https://www.apidura.com/journal/parallel-plans-2022/. If interested in hiring a cycling guide on the Shimanami, contact Taro at @sc_dreamin 

The Shimanami Kaido Association website:  https://shimanami-cycle.or.jp/cycling/en-02.html

YouTube Channel page with #shorts and regular videos:  https://www.youtube.com/c/MikeMatsuno

Best Rice in the World:  https://youtu.be/QFyfOB6vyMs
Japan Bath: https://youtu.be/tPyJliDAm4Q
Japanese curry:  https://youtu.be/FFseCI802R4

Please subscribe:  https://www.youtube.com/MikeMatsuno​
Mike's website:  https://www.goglobal.me/​.

#japantravel  #japantrip #japantravelvideo
tags: cycling in japan 10 of the best rides

videos.title: A Japan MUST: Cycling the Shimanami Kaido!
videos.description:
The Shimanami Kaido is a 70km ride that connects Honshu to Shikoku via a series of picturesque islands. This slice of Japanese paradise is packed with gorgeous vistas, ample opportunities to explore local cuisine and culture, and is safe and well signposted. In this video I take you through what you need to know and what you can expect on the ride!

===============================

Helpful Links From the Video

General

Bike Rental Info: https://shimanami-cycle.or.jp/rental/english

Matsushige Liquor Shop on Google Maps: https://goo.gl/maps/VUK615mr4nsQ8nD88

Omishima Island

incline getting too steep
there are actually multiple ways to
approach the ride there's a recommended
route for cyclists at basically any
level but there are often intermediate
and even Advanced options too how you
approach the shimanami kaido then
depends on how fit you are how much
writing experience you have what bike
you'll be using and how much cycling
gear you earn I don't cycle very often
and the bike I rented wasn't really up
to the task of dealing with anything too
challenging so for the most part I
stuffed the recommended path with a few
detours out of curiosity thrown in
my bike may not have been a Precision
piece of modern high-tech engineering
but the bike rental prices are pretty
good they're currently 3 000 Yen per day
for a mama Charlie or a cross bike four
thousand yen per day for a tandem or
electric assist bike and 8 000 Yen per
day for an e-bike bear in mind however
that only the 3 000 Yen options can be
used across multiple days everything
else is the same day rental whether you
need to reserve a bike ahead of time
probably depends on when you're doing
the ride you're definitely better off
being safe than sorry if you're tackling
it on a public holiday or during a busy
time of year but when I went to the
rental terminal the day before my ride
they just told me to get there early the
next day and I'd be fine that's it if
you're not going through the shiman Army
rental Cycle Service and instead want to
get your hands on say a proper road bike
you'll 100 want to tee that up in
advance
again it all depends on your experience
level and expectations I found the basic
cross bike perfectly fine for the
entirety of the recommended route and it
was also good knowing that if anything
went wrong I could call shimanami Japan
or visit one of the bike terminals for
help a company called Wakka also offers
both a roadside repair service and taxi
pickup one other thing to know there are
more than 200 cycle Oasis stops along
the Route while they vary in terms of
services the basic idea is that you can
use them to take a break refill your
water bottles and use pumps if necessary
oh and be sure to bring plenty of cash
you can pay for lots of stuff with cards
in the big Japanese cities but out on
this course cash is still King with all
that out of the way let's start the
journey
I picked up my bag from the render cycle
terminal at onamichi Port when it opened
which was 7 AM March November and 8 A.M
December to February from there it's a
short ride to get the ferry across the
water to mukashima Island to start the
ride proper the ferry costs 100 yen and
you'd pay on board once off we'll be
introduced to one of the greatest
features of the shimanami kaido cycle
route the blue line yes if you're taking
the recommended route all you have to do
is follow the blue line and it'll take
you all the way from Panchu to shikoku
or vice versa no stopping to check Maps
or phones just follow the line the
journey starts out along relatively
Urban streets but it's not too long
before you're beside the water and going
under the bright red Muka Ashima Bridge
which leads to owashu Island beyond that
we'll get a look at the first of six
Bridges who are set right across in
noshima Bridge is 1.27 kilometers long
and takes you to inoshima Island
to get onto the bridge you actually need
to go under it and then loop back around
this will become a familiar part of this
cycle starting at sea level and steadily
climbing along narrow bike paths until
you're at the level of the bridge or
close to it in this case as the bike and
pedestrian path across inoshima bridge
is actually beneath the expressway for
other vehicles this means your viewers
curtailed somewhat but it also makes it
nice and leisurely to cross coming back
down on the other side you'll soon see
the inoshima amenity Park and its star
attraction meet zarukun why is there a
large presumably albino dinosaur here I
couldn't find an adequate explanation
online but who am I to judge I live in a
country that seems to think that random
big things are the premier tourist
destination
be nice if it was a bit more realistic
these should all have feathers right the
park also has a playground pool and
Beach and if you backtrack towards the
bridge a bit you can pop into hasakuya
it's named after the citrus fruit hasaku
which was developed in inoshima and the
shop sells Mochi with pieces of hasaku
inside as well as a range of other sweet
treats
and speaking of hasaku this is hassakun
the inevitable mascot based on said
Citrus honestly no other country churns
out mascots with anywhere near the
efficiency or Ingenuity of Japan
I mean check out sukimamori a J.R West's
mascot monster that lurks in the gap
between the train and platform waiting
for kids who don't mind the gap and fall
down what happens then I hate to think
oh wait it's friendly
of course it is in fact apparently it
dances when it's happy likes to play
hide and seek and is free-spirited
Whimsical and Moody created to raise
awareness of the dangers of kids falling
in train station gaps this mascot is
classic Japan this is a country that's
both extremely worried about people
hurting themselves and absolutely
fantastic at making characters emerge
out of just about anything the
recommended route on inoshima island
takes you through its northern end and
this is mostly Suburban but popped with
Gardens and greenhouses and with some
pretty backdrops thanks to the mountains
in the distance one of these mountains
shuritaki has 700 Buddha statues at its
Summit and commanding 360 degree views
of the region including ikuchi Island
and the impressive Bridge leading across
to it getting up there on the bike
definitely falls into the advanced
category but you can potentially leave
it chained up somewhere below and walk
off
as a quick aside Hiroshima is famous for
its okonomiyaki so it's no surprise in
Oshima has its own spin on the classic
dish known as inoko a combination of
inoshima and okonomiyaki it uses udon
noodles instead of soba before long
you'll be riding along the coast again
with the next Bridge inside but it's
here that the traffic steps up and
things get a whole lot busier on the
plus side there are three or four
combinis in a short stretch so if you're
running dangerously low on femi chickies
this may be a good point on the trip to
stock for a snack and drink or just to
stock up on suppliers once again as you
approach a coochie Bridge you'll go
under it then loop back for the climb up
be sure to keep an eye out for the sign
to turn off as it's easy to miss and
you'll suddenly wonder where the Blue
Line went just like this
there's a nice rest area on the ascent
with fantastic views across to the
bridge in akuchi Island as well as off
the water and iwagi island in the
distance
the impressive cable stayed ekuchi
bridge is one of the shorter bridges on
the ride only 790 meters long and unlike
in the Shimmer Bridge you're on the same
level as the traffic but with your own
section of the road once you reach its
end there's a great cruise down a
winding path to reach the cycle route
once again
these descents after each Bridge came to
be one of my favorite parts of moving
from Mounds Island
the recommended path through ikuchi
Island follows the coast North and stays
on or close to the coast the whole way
this island is dense with Citrus and
lemon Orchards to the point that it's
known as lemon Island and is
unsurprisingly also chock full of shops
selling lemons and lemon related
products you know the old saying when
life hands you lemons name your Island
after them and also make a commemorative
ventral tube
staying on the subject of the local
lemons there's a gelato factory and Cafe
called setoda Dolce on the way so I
stopped off for a fantastically Zippy
and fresh lemon gelato according to the
Shop's website translated by Google I
might add so let's take it with a grain
of salt dolce's special Citrus is grown
With Love by Mr Mourinho a citrus
Craftsman from Saturday The Taste and
credibility are the highest grade Citrus
sold in department stores kind of wish
I'd become a citrus Craftsman now
instead of an amateur sake taster
although I guess we both have a
commitment to deliciousness the island
of ikuchi is actually one of the most
popular tourist destinations of the ride
and the small town of setodar which is a
little further along the northern Shore
from Dolce Gelato has a number of
attractions call Sanji is one of the
most notable and consists of a temple
complex Museum and sculptural garden
made from Marble called the hill of Hope
among other things at 1400 yen to enter
it was a bit Rich for my tastes and it's
also worth pointing out that corsanji is
pretty modern the temple is from the
1930s onwards while the hill of Hope
opened in the year 2000 even so it's a
good option
there's also the hirayama ikawa museum
of art named after and dedicated to the
work of the famous contemporary painter
hiroyama is perhaps best known for his
pieces inspired by the Silk Road but for
shimanami cyclists the museum also hosts
a collection called 53 stages on the
shimanami highway which includes 60
watercolor drawings and three paintings
showcasing sites along the Route entry
to the museum is 1000 yen for adults I
also climbed a hill to reach the
three-story Pagoda of kokoji Temple this
National Treasure is a hugely impressive
structure and dates back to the 15th
century you also get a nice Outlook over
the distant mountains and nearby port
by this point I'd build up quite an
appetite so I headed to shilmachi's
shortened guy or shumachi shopping
street to see what was on offer despite
some nice looking restaurants and cafes
I ultimately couldn't resist the lure of
matsushige liquor shop after all what
better combination could there be than a
booze Emporium that also serves up
Hiroshima style okonomiyaki
it turned out to be a great choice with
a super friendly owner bizarre and
quirky decor and food that was both
delicious and incredible value I'll drop
the website link in Google map location
in the description if you're Keen to
check it out once my Hunger was sighted
I explored the immediate area a little
discovering as is the way in Japan
Australian and a couple of temples then
continued on my way the recommended path
takes you from set a dark horse down the
Island's Western Edge to tatara bridge
which then leads to all Mishima Island
tatara Bridge is like the big brother of
akuchi bridge and is almost twice as
long coming in at 1.48 kilometers it
also marks the point on the ride that
you cross over from Hiroshima prefecture
and into ahime prefecture that happens
right about here
ordinarily you'd come down off the
bridge and head south but my
accommodation for the night was just to
the north so I looped around in that
direction
where did I stay Eileen hostel and Cafe
the facilities are modern and clean and
you can choose between lobbying your
bike and the undercover shed outside or
simply chucking it on the wall of your
room on top of that there's a Family
Mart across the road and a bath house
just down the street let's quickly talk
about said bathhouse it's in this funky
building the shiman Army dome which also
houses a swimming pool gym and
basketball court the bathing facilities
feel like an old school Cento with one
large hot bath inside divided into two
sections one side has a series of jets
to massage your back the other a step to
sit on there's also a small sauna with
wooden seats and no mats as well as a
cold bath which wasn't quite cold enough
outside there's a tiny patio area with
another bar if you stand up you have a
great view of the nearby Bridge mountain
range and water but as soon as you sit
down for a soak you can only see the
very top of the bridge somewhat
defeating the purpose of having an
outdoor area still for 420 Yen admission
and 30 Yen for soap it's not a bad deal
be sure to bring your own towel though
after my soak I grabbed a meal at the
hostel and sampled some local sake and
called it a night oyasumina Sai
omishima is easily the largest of the
islands of the shimanamikato goes
through but the recommended path barely
touches it if you've got the time and a
good enough fight you may be interested
in taking one of the routes around the
outside of the island if you follow the
intermediate path to the north you can
actually get a ferry from sakuri Port to
okono Shima AKA Rabbit Island this
diminutive destination has literally
hundreds of rabbits roaming around
making it a particularly cute place to
visit with kids mind you it does have a
messed up past as okanoshima was the
center of Japan's chemical weapons
research and production during World War
II and at that time was raised from the
map entirely there's now a poison gas
Museum you can visit as well as other
remnants of the Island's dark past
speaking of remnants the official
website says that the rabbits may have
been introduced in the aftermath of
World War II in order to identify poison
gas leaks Canary in a coal mine Style
in any case along with feeding rabbits
you can also walk around the islands
four kilometers circumference hike up to
the observation platform at its Center
or have a swim through where the
swarming off returning to omoshima
Island the advanced path along the
southern coast is meant to offer some
incredible views with even the kuroshima
kaiko bridges and emabari visible on
clear days
nope it's a long path but you're riding
beside picturesque Citrus Orchards and
the road is reportedly good with hills
that are steady as opposed to steep
alongside a handful of modern art and
architecture museums the oyamazumi
shrine nestled beneath Mount washigato
is a key attraction of the island as it
has one of the world's greatest
collections of Samurai armor and swords
why well according to the shimanami
Japan website it was one of the most
important shrines in the country for
centuries it was so notable that
high-ranking Samurai would come to a
yamazumi to pray for success in battle
if they proved Victorious they would
then return and offer their armor and
weapons to the shrine hence the
incredible collection the shrine also
has a sacred tree in front of the main
hall that's over 2 600 years old Legend
has it that if you complete three laps
around the tree while holding your
breath your wish will come true
I really hope the samurai captains did
this too but somehow I doubt it
the oyama Zumi Shrine can be reached by
either of the paths around the outside
of the island but also via the route
that cuts straight through to mirport
if you're just following the recommended
route on the other hand one thing to
look out for is the amazaki castle ruins
on kojo island which are all that's left
of a castle complex that was here from
the sengoku period or Warring States
period through to the early Edo nowadays
only some remnants of the stone walls
remain
there's also plenty to discover if you
want to take small detours but the
recommended path very quickly reaches
the shortest bridge of them all and
takes you onto Hakata this island has a
number of attractions such as beaches
views and shrines but I decided to go
under the next bridge and follow the
intermediate path to reach the funaori
Seto straight stunning deck as it's
labeled on the official map
before that however there are shrines to
detour by and a good cycling rest stop
just next to Hakata beach with this palm
tree Sandy Shore in view of the next
Bridge it's the ideal spot to grab a
coffee in a can or a bottle of water and
to chill out for a bit
moving on I soon reached the observation
deck and it was well worth the journey
with a fantastic view to nearby ushima
Island and across to orshima
and have a look at how strong the
current is the sign as I came off the
bridge described this as a whirling
tidal current and one of Nature's
Mysteries there were some nice views of
the Hakata or Shimmer Bridge as I headed
back towards it and it wasn't long until
I was on the bridge itself enjoying how
open it is just over a kilometer cycling
later and I was on to the last Island on
the shimanami kaido oh Shima all
Shimmer's recommended path starts out
along the coast and it was on this
stretch that I decided lunch was in
order so popped into a completely random
restaurant by the water for some
Yakitori I then detoured to visit ibisu
the god of fishermen and delicious beer
at miyokubo Port then double back to
follow the route inland
as much as I love cycling beside the
water this next section was incredibly
picturesque with Gorgeous traditional
houses at the base of Rocky heavily
forested mountains dotted with cherry
trees
found myself turning off the main road a
few times just to ride through small
towns to soak up the atmosphere
the recommended route through all Shuma
includes two long climbs so it can be a
challenging way to end the ride for some
the alternative is to take the
intermediate path along the western
coast for much longer but steadier cycle
on the other hand those who want to
really push themselves can Branch off
from the recommended path for a grueling
3.5 kilometer ride to the top of Mount
Kiro
the reward for doing this is one of the
best views on the entire shimanami kaido
all good things must come to an end
however and it's not long before the
final set of bridges is inside and boy
are these impressive the kuroshima kaiko
bridges are the world's longest series
of suspension bridges more than four
kilometers to Traverse with numerous
small Islands beneath and nearby as you
ride
when you come to the end of the bridges
you should detour to the right and visit
the kuroshima street Observatory for
absolutely epic views back across the
bridges to Oma Shimmer as well as of the
various nearby Islands
The itoyama Observatory is also super
close with a higher viewing platform and
great Vista that even lets you see the
cyclists coming off the bridges and
looping around as they descend
from here it's actually possible to drop
your bike off at the nearby sunrise
itoyama
these are machines machines were for
weaving kimono no this is a vehicle
called a bicycle you can ride it all
over the shimanami it's fantastic
but I was staying the night in imabari
so Road into the city to the rental bike
terminal at Jr imabari station imabari
is a biggest city so this part of the
journey was very busy and very Urban and
in fact it was the only time in the two
days of cycling that I rode on the
footpath to get away from the traffic
in any case drop-off was Simplicity
itself and I was soon free to soothe my
slightly fatigued muscles at a bath
house located only a couple of blocks
away was kisuke no you an Onsen as well
as capsule hotel that very much caters
to shimanami kaido Riders using the
Onsen costs between 650 and 750 Yen
depending on when you go or you can pay
around double that to also access the
rest area Bedrock bath and for all you
can drink coffee
I paid to use the Onsen only and it was
great with plenty of options
there are tons of different types of
Bays with jets to massage your muscles a
large carbonated bath with denkiburu or
electric bath Bays watch my Onsen
etiquette video for more on those a
walking bath that's reputed to be good
for rehabilitation a semi-open air
alkaline rock bath where I spent some
time watching sumo wrestling on TV a
bath for kids filled with rubber duckies
and a shower bucket that lets you pull a
cord and dump water on yourself it also
has a good sized sauna and a tiny wet
sauna that's only big enough for four
and has chill atmospheric music oh and a
large coal bar the women's area also has
a jade bath using soft water that
supposedly has the moisturizing effect
on the skin according to the Google
translation of the website this bath
will let you feel the power of Jade
which has a powerful healing effect that
is said to stabilize the mind
the bathing area was pretty packed when
I was there but it was still well worth
visiting the iron the walls is an
awesome Touch Too whether you're in the
men's section with its ghetto meets the
modern era on the shimanami kaido vibe
or in the women's with its tail of a
girl riding the shimanami kaido to
deliver mandarins to mermaids Cuppa and
Celestial maidens after my soak I
grabbed a cheap and cheerful bowl of
udon in the restaurant and set off to
find my accommodation for the night
aryakan over near the ferry terminal to
say it was old school would be a massive
understatement but hey it certainly had
personality
so that was my experience cycling the
shiman Army kaido it was an awesome
couple of days and I hope this video
inspires you to hop on a bike and do the
same the next time you're in Japan
please give the vid a like And subscribe
to the channel and stay tuned for more
recommendations for hikes and day trips
in the Land of the Rising fun and I'll
leave you with more mascot Madness
um
okay
videos.curation_reason: Recent comprehensive ride experience with detailed practical information and first-person perspective.

videos.title: 🚴🎵30min Indoor cycling scenery | Shimanami Kaido① [cycling Japan]
videos.description:
Indoor cycling training, cycling for Weight loss & Refreshment for your health.
Let's get started with me !🚴
※This trip is 30minutes X 6 Challenge Videos. 
and 15min time table in each videos.  Keep your pace & complete this trip !!

Location : Imabari city (Ehime prefecture) -- Onomichi city (Hiroshima Pref) 
Date : 2022/6
Distance: 74 ㎞　Elevation:  420m
Route: Main Shimanami cycling road that pass through 6 islands and 6 bridges.
 
Give me any comments, your experience & requests.
Your comment is BIG support for this channel. 💪
  
Please subscribe this channel. 👍‍
Get healthier and happier together :D

For stationary bike, exercise bike, Spinning bike, Trainer, Rollers, Turbo trainer and armchair traveler.

BAD:私が少し下を向くと顔が、、、走行姿勢が変えずらい事と少しカメラの方向が下向きでブレが大きいかな？
どちらもそれぞれメリット、デメリットがありますが、どちらが好きですか？

quotes.text: When I hit the first Bridge called inoshima Bridge from mukashima Island to inoshima Island this past Monday I felt like I hit a wall and I had just started. The upward inclines to climb to the top where you can take the suspension bridges over to the next Island are very tough and grueling depending on your bike your physical shape stamina and age.

This 70 kilometer ride connects Honshu to shikoku Via a series of picturesque Islands. The journey offers up incredible views ample opportunities to explore local Cuisine and culture and is safe and well signposted.

key_challenges.name: Inoshima Bridge approach
key_challenges.description: The upward inclines to climb to the bridges are very tough and grueling. One rider felt like they hit a wall on the first bridge approach from Mukoshima Island to Inoshima Island and had just started.

key_challenges.name: Bridge approaches
key_challenges.description: Every bridge has bespoke approaches for bikes that gradually climb to bridge height, but the elevation changes are still challenging depending on fitness level and bike type.

terrain_notes.text: The flat areas are not so difficult, but the upward inclines to the bridges are very tough and grueling. The cycle route takes a scenic path winding around coastlines rather than punching straight through islands.

gear_mentions.text: Choose your bike wisely - a mama chari (standard bike with three gears) was not easy for the inclines. High-tech bicycles would not have such problems, but they're not comfortable and don't have baskets or racks for carrying things.

E-bikes are suggested for those not in good shape, older, or inexperienced - the battery should last for one full day (8-10 hours from Onomichi to Imabari) and can get partial recharge at Omishima halfway point.

race_day_tips.text: Manage your expectations carefully - it's better to underestimate how far you can cycle rather than overestimating. The ride can take 4-5 hours for fast cyclists or 8-10 hours for beginners.
Don't cycle with a heavy load on your back as it's not good for cycling and can upset your balance when steering.

additional_quotes.text: You can put your luggage in a locker if returning to starting point, or use the cycling without baggage service to forward luggage to hotels, hostels and ryokan for 2,200 yen - found it straightforward and reasonable value.

E-bikes must be returned to the rental shop you rented from at the end of the day - you cannot go overnight as the battery will not last and you won't have a charger. If you return at another shop you forfeit your deposit.

Riding in summer heat and humidity sounds awful, winter isn't ideal either - this is most likely a spring or autumn ride. March weather was great for riding - crisp mornings but nice once you get going.

rider_intel.search_text: The Shimanami Kaido is a 70-kilometer coastal cycling route connecting Honshu to Shikoku via six islands and suspension bridges. Riders describe the flat sections as manageable but warn that bridge approaches involve tough, grueling climbs that can feel like hitting a wall, especially on the first Inoshima Bridge approach. The route winds scenically around coastlines rather than cutting straight through islands, with bespoke bike approaches to each bridge. Standard bikes struggle on inclines while high-tech bikes perform better but lack storage. E-bikes are recommended for less experienced riders, lasting 8-10 hours with partial recharge available at Omishima halfway point. The ride takes 4-5 hours for fast cyclists or 8-10 hours for beginners. Spring and autumn are ideal seasons, avoiding summer heat and winter conditions. Riders emphasize managing expectations carefully and choosing appropriate bikes for fitness levels.
